coredata.dc:title: Fe-doping green fluorescent carbon dots via co-electrolysis of chrysoidine G and potassium ferrocyanide for sensitive Cr(VI) detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Long, Tiantian
Luo, Hongmei
Li, Hongchen
Sun, Jingbo
Wang, Yang
Zhou, Jiaquan
Chen, Yi
Xu, Dong
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we aimed to synthesis of Fe-doping green fluorescent carbon dots (G-CDs) through the co-electrolysis of chrysoidine G and potassium ferrocyanide for Cr(VI) detection. The use of potassium ferrocyanide improves the quantum yield and sensing performance of G-CDs toward Cr(VI). The G-CDs have a maximum excitation wavelength of 308 nm and an emission wavelength of 510 nm. Comprehensive analyses including Raman, FT-IR, and XPS provided insights into the chemical structure and composition of the G-CDs. Under optimal conditions, G-CDs demonstrated concentration-dependent quenching upon interaction with Cr(VI). A linear relationship within the range of 0.25–100 µM was established with a calibration equation of ΔF/F0 = 0.005 + 0.015CCr(VI), yielding an R2 value of 0.996 and a limit of detection of 0.15 μM. The applicability of the G-CDs method was demonstrated by successful Cr(VI) detection in water samples with recovery rates ranging from 98.8 % to 100.1 % and relative standard deviation within 3.0 %. The fluorescence lifetime and Zeta potential measurements confirmed that the mechanism was via a static quenching process, while redox reaction, nanoparticle aggregation, and surface charge variation also played significant roles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green fluorescence
Carbon dots
Co-electrolysis
Chromium (VI)
Quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical synthesis and optimization of G-CDs
Detection of Cr(VI) by using G-CDs
The mechanism for Cr(VI) sensing by G-CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by funding from the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21505162 , 31571874 ), Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province , China ( 2020RC3042 , 2022SK2102 ), Hainan Province Science and Technology Special Fund 396 (Grant No. ZDYF2020114 ), Hunan Provincial Department of Education Scientific Research Project ( 240994 ), Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Safety Monitoring and Early Warning (No. 2020KFJJ05 ), the Grain-Oil Process and Quality Control 2011 Collaborative and Innovative Grant from Hunan province, and Training Program for Excellent Young Innovators of Changsha (kq1802043). We also gratefully acknowledge financial support from China Scholarship Council .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Fluorescence spectra of G-CDs electrolytically prepared from chrysidine G and increasing concentrations of potassium ferrocyanide. The mass fraction of G-CDs is 0.014 %. (b) Fluorescence intensity (left) and quantum yield (QY, right) of G-CDs prepared with and without the presence of potassium ferrocyanide over the range of 0–5.0 mM (EX = 308 nm;EM = 507 nm). Insets are the images of G-CD solution under the illumination of 365 nm UV lamp prepared with the presence of 0 and 0.75 mM potassium ferrocyanide as the representatives. Table at the right listed the QY of G-CDs prepared under various potassium ferrocyanide. (c) Schematic preparation of G-CDs by co-electrolysis of chrysoidine G and potassium ferrocyanide.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence excitation and emission spectrum of G-CDs. Insets are the color diagrams of the G-CD solution under natural light (left) and 365 nm UV lamp. (b) HRTEM image and particle size distribution statistics. (c) Raman spectrum. (d) FT-IR spectra of G-CDs and the raw materials (chrysoidine G + potassium ferrocyanide).
(a) Total X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). (b-e) High-resolution XPS of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, (d) O1s, and (e)Fe2p.
Fluorescence variation of G-CDs after reaction with Cr(VI) at different pH conditions (a), at different temperatures (b), and different time (c).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of G-CDs with Cr(VI) concentration at 0–1000 μM under the optimal conditions. (b) ΔF/ F0 vs Cr(VI) concentration (Inset shows the color variation of the solution under 365 nm UV light.)
The selectivity of G-CDs toward Cr(VI) over other ions.

Characterization of G-CDs after reaction with Cr(VI). (a) FT-IR spectrum. (b) XPS total spectrum. (c-g)High resolution XPS of (c) C1s, (d) N1s, (e) O1s, (f) Fe2p, and (g) Cr2p. (h) Fluorescence lifetime. (i) Particle size. (j) Zeta potential.

head.ce:title: Fe-doping green fluorescent carbon dots via co-electrolysis of chrysoidine G and potassium ferrocyanide for sensitive Cr(VI) detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we aimed to synthesis of Fe-doping green fluorescent carbon dots (G-CDs) through the co-electrolysis of chrysoidine G and potassium ferrocyanide for Cr(VI) detection. The use of potassium ferrocyanide improves the quantum yield and sensing performance of G-CDs toward Cr(VI). The G-CDs have a maximum excitation wavelength of 308 nm and an emission wavelength of 510 nm. Comprehensive analyses including Raman, FT-IR, and XPS provided insights into the chemical structure and composition of the G-CDs. Under optimal conditions, G-CDs demonstrated concentration-dependent quenching upon interaction with Cr(VI). A linear relationship within the range of 0.25–100 µM was established with a calibration equation of ΔF/F0 = 0.005 + 0.015CCr(VI), yielding an R2 value of 0.996 and a limit of detection of 0.15 μM. The applicability of the G-CDs method was demonstrated by successful Cr(VI) detection in water samples with recovery rates ranging from 98.8 % to 100.1 % and relative standard deviation within 3.0 %. The fluorescence lifetime and Zeta potential measurements confirmed that the mechanism was via a static quenching process, while redox reaction, nanoparticle aggregation, and surface charge variation also played significant roles.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The determination of chromium levels in food and water sources holds paramount importance in safeguarding human health and promoting the harmony of natural ecosystems and societal development [1–3]. The intensification of large-scale industrialization has exacerbated the issue of heavy metal such as chromium pollution [4]. On the other hand, chromium is widely used in various industrial processes, such as electroplating, leather tanning, and pigment production [5], which can occasionally result in its release into the environment. Among all chromium species, Cr(VI) is the most toxic and its exposure can lead to a range of adverse health effects, including lung cancer, liver damage, kidney damage, and reproductive problems [6,7]. Cr(VI) contamination of soil and water sources may affect human health and ecosystems. Therefore, its detection and quantification are crucial for environmental monitoring, occupational safety, and public health [8]. US EPA suggested that the concentration of Cr(VI) in drinking water should be lower than 100 ppb [9].

Due to the toxicity of Cr(VI), various mature detection methods have been developed, including inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [10,11], atomic absorption spectroscopy [12], chromatography [13], and inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES) [14], which are capable of detecting Cr(VI) in water media. However, these methods are hindered by expensive instruments, high costs, and unsuitabilityfor field use. In contrast, the fluorescent method has the advantages of simple sample pretreatment, fast response, wide linear dynamic range, strong anti-interference ability, and high sensitivity, which can meet the requirements of industrial applications [15,16]. Carbon dots (CDs), as excellent fluorescent sensing materials [17–20], offer a promising alternative for Cr(VI) detection. Several reports have described methods utilizing CDs for Cr(VI) detection. For instance, Feng et al., [21] synthesized CDs using kelp as the carbon source via a hydrothermal method, achieving fluorescence quenching due to the inner filter effect (IFE) between the surface functional groups of CDs and Cr(VI), enabling its detection within the range of 0.01–50 μM with a detection limit as low as 0.52 μM. Zhang et al., [22] successfully analyzed real water samples using nitrogen-doped CDs based on the IFE and static quenching effect. Red-emissive CDs were rapidly prepared via a microwave method for Cr(VI) determination with a limit of detection of 0.9 μM [23]. The blue-emissive CDs obtained by heating the mixture of trimesic acid, urea, and polyethylene diamine branched have a good response to Cr(VI) within the range of 0.5–1.3 μM [18]. However, nearly all these CDs are prepared by a hydrothermal or microwave approach, which consume a lot of energy and time. Recently, the electrochemical method for CD fabrication has emerged as a rapid, simple, and energy-saving strategy, but the as-prepared CDs usually exhibit inferior optical properties such as low quantum yield. Such research gap needs to be filled by developing new tactics.

This study focuses on the synthesis and characterization of Fe-doping green fluorescent carbon dots (G-CDs) through the co-electrolysis of chrysoidine G and potassium ferrocyanide, which were further applied for Cr(VI) detection. The introduction of potassium ferrocyanide during electrolysis can effectively enhance quantum yield and sensing performance of G-CDs toward Cr(VI). The chemical structure and composition of the G-CDs with the optimum optical performance were characterized by using Raman, FT-IR, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). G-CDs were efficiently applied for Cr(VI) detection based on a quenching mechanism. The method was validated by successful Cr(VI) detection in water samples with high recovery rates and low relative standard deviation. This study provides valuable insights into the development of electrochemically fabricated CDs with outstanding performance for environmental monitoring applications.

Chrysoidine G (4-phenylazo-m-phenylenediamine monohydrochloride, C12H12N4•HCl), o-phenylenediamine, 1,2,4-triaminobenzene, potassium ferrocyanide, and sodium dihydrogen phosphate were bought from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (China). Potassium dichromate, ammonium sulfate, ferric chloride, and calcium chloride were obtained from Aladdin biochemical technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Copper sulfate pentahydrate, silver nitrate, potassium bromide, sodium hydroxide, nickel nitrate hexahydrate, cadmium chloride, aluminium chloride hexahydrate, and lithium sulfate were purchased from Sinopharm Group Co Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Phosphate buffered saline (PBS, 1 × ) was bought from Jinuo Biochemical Co., Ltd (Haining, China).

A high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, Titan G2 60–300, FEI, Hillsboro, OR, USA) was used to obtain the morphology of G-CD. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were scanned on a Tracer-100 (Shimadzu, Japan) spectrophotometer. UV–Vis spectra were recorded on a spectrophotometer (UV-2700, Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were obtained by using G9800A (Agilent, Santa Clara, CA, USA). The fluorescence lifetime of the G-CDs was acquired on steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (FLS1000, Edinburgh Instruments, Edinburgh, Scotland). Zeta potential analyzer (Nano-ZS90, Malvern, UK) was used to obtain the diameter and zeta potential. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained on a K-Alpha multichine (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Wilmington, MA, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical synthesis and optimization of G-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Chrysoidine G was dissolved in PBS buffer solution (0.2 mM, pH = 7) to achieve a final concentration of 0.73 mM. Subsequently, 13.5 mL of the stock solution was mixed with 1.5 mL of various concentrations of potassium ferrocyanide (0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0, and 5.0 mM) and subjected to electrolysis for 12 min in an electrolysis device, operating at the voltage of 10 V [24]. The device was a bespoke apparatus (Kaixin Co., Ltd, Taiwan), consisting of a voltage transformer (220 V-10 V), a 25-mL bottle, and two platinum electrodes with 4 cm in length and 0.1 cm in diameter, which were fixed on the cap. The resulting G-CDs were dialyzed for 12 h using dialysis bags (MW cutoff = 500) to remove residual PBS buffer, unreacted chrysoidine G, and potassium ferrocyanide. Subsequently the G-CDs were subjected to UV–visible and fluorescence spectrophotometer scanning. The G-CDs displaying the strongest fluorescence were selected for further characterization and sensing applications. A 10 μL droplet of the purified solution was deposited onto a carbon film and observed using HR-TEM. The purified G-CDs powder obtained after freeze-drying was used for FT-IR and Raman spectroscopy. 10 μL of the solution was dried on a silicon wafer for XPS measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cr(VI) by using G-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The purified G-CDs solution was diluted tenfold for Cr(VI) detection. In a plastic centrifuge tube containing 2.5 mL of G-CDs solution, 0.5 mL of PBS buffer was added to create a weakly alkaline environment. Various amounts of Cr(VI) solution were then added, and after thorough mixing, the solution was subjected to fluorescence spectrum scanning with an excitation wavelength at 344 nm. The emission spectra in the range of 400–600 nm were scanned with a slit width of 5.0 and a voltage of 700 V. To achieve the best performance for Cr(VI) sensing, optimizations of pH value, temperature, and time were performed. A standard curve was established using fluorescence ratio ΔF/F0. Interferences from other were studied by replacing the Cr(VI) with 100 μM of Co2+, Fe3+, Li+, Zn2+, NH4 +, Cu2+, Sn2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Ag+, Mn2+, Cr3+, Ni2+, Cd2+, and Al3+.

Tap water, directly collected in the lab, was detected by using G-CDs as the probe. Spiked samples were prepared by adding 15, 50, and 100 μM Cr(VI) solution. For each sample, 0.5 mL of the water sample was mixed with 2.5 mL of G-CDs solution and 0.5 mL of pH 8 PBS buffer in sequence. The fluorescence emission spectra of the mixtures were tested, with an excitation wavelength of 344 nm. All the tests were repeated three times, and the recovery rate was calculated.

Potassium ferrocyanide is commonly used as an electrochemical probe for studying the mechanism and kinetics of electrochemical reactions [25]. It was discovered that using PBS as the electrolyte and co-electrolyzing chrysoidine G and potassium ferrocyanide can enhance the fluorescence performance of G-CDs. The concentration of potassium ferrocyanide was investigated within the range of 0–5.0 mM. From Fig. 1 a, it can be observed that when chrysoidine G was electrolyzed alone, G-CD were obtained with an emission wavelength at 510 nm under the excitation wavelength at 308 nm. As the concentration of potassium ferrocyanide increased from 0 to 5.0 mM, the intensity showed an increasing-then-decreasing trend (Fig. 1b). It peaked at 0.75 mM, showing a 6.9-fold increase in fluorescence compared to G-CDs prepared without potassium ferrocyanide. The G-CD solution under this condition exhibited strong green fluorescence, which was opted for further applications. According to previous report, the formation of G-CDs involves the processes including dehydration, polymerization, carbonization, and passivation when co-electrolyzing potassium ferrocyanide and chrysoidine G at the anode [15,26]. The fluorescence enhancement is attributed to Fe doping in the presence of potassium ferrocyanide. Additionally, potassium ferrocyanide, as an electrolyte in electrochemistry, may enhance the carbonization and passivation degree of G-CDs [15,26]. Fig. 1c presents the schematic diagram of the electrochemical preparation of G-CDs doped with Fe using chrysoidine G as the carbon source.

Characterization of G-CDs was conducted using UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 2 a). After dilution of G-CDs tenfold, a broad absorption peak was observed at 395 nm in the UV absorption spectrum, possibly due to n → π* transition [27]. The G-CDs solution has a maximum excitation wavelength at 308 nm and an emission wavelength at 510 nm. The significant disparities observed between the UV–Vis adsorption spectrum and the fluorescence excitation curve can be attributed to the unique nature of G-CDs. These nanomaterials possess distinct adsorption and fluorescence centers that are spatially separated. The inset shows that the G-CDs solution is yellow under natural light and emitted strong green fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. The quantum yield (QY) for G-CDs can achieve 6.05 %, while the QY of CDs solely derived from chrysoidine G is as low as 0.87 %. The approach proves highly valuable for electrochemical preparation of CDs with superior optical performance and the QY surpasses that of the majority of CDs prepared without the use of potassium ferrocyanide [15,26]. Following similar method [18], we obtained the HOMO and LUMO energy levels. The HOMO values for CDs, G-CDs, and Fe3+ are −5.94 ev, −5.95 ev, and −5.08 ev, while the LUMO values are −2.20 ev, −2.38 ev, and −2.40 ev, respectively. After doping, the energy gap was reduced and HOMO and LUMO orbital energy was downward shifted, conducive to electronic transition and enhancing photoluminescence, based on the intersystem crossing between G-CDs and transition metal ions [28].

HRTEM was employed for directly observing the morphology of G-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2b, the particle size of G-CDs has a narrow distribution, and these fluorescent G-CDs could disperse in water freely. Analysis based on data from 150 particles revealed that the sizes of the G-CDs ranged from 3 to 5 nm. The Raman spectrum (Fig. 2c) displayed a peak at 1562 cm−1 (G-band), representing sp2 carbon in the system with in-plane vibrations of C-C bonds. The approximately 1371 cm−1 (D-band) revealed the presence of sp3-hybridized carbon in G-CDs, originating from out-of-plane vibrations of C-C bonds. The value of ID/IG is larger than 1.0, implying the majority of amorphous carbon in G-CDs as well as a higher oxidation degree, more substitution groups, and more surface functional groups with defects [29].

In FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2d), the G-CDs exhibited stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H with a broad peak at approximately 3421 cm−1 [30]. Absorption peaks at 2451 cm−1 and 1641 cm−1 corresponded to C-O and C = C vibrations, respectively, confirming the presence of C = C after the carbonization [30,31]. The peak at 1265 cm−1 represented asymmetric and symmetric stretching modes of C-O-C, demonstrating that oxygen-functional groups such as C-O and C-O-C in the carbon skeleton edges are probably the reason for emission in G-CDs [32,33]. Peaks at 400–600 cm−1 were related to Fe-O bonds [34], indicating the formation of carbon/oxygen-rich groups on the surface of G-CDs.

XPS further characterized the structural composition of G-CDs (Fig. 3 ). In the full spectrum of G-CDs (Fig. 3a), four major peaks were observed at 284.4, 398.2, 531.2, and 708.6 eV, corresponding to the binding energies of C1s, N1s, O1s, and Fe2p, with elemental ratios of 17.06 %, 5.78 %, 76.29 %, and 0.87 %, respectively [35]. The N element was from chrysoidine G, while Fe2p had the lowest proportion in G-CDs, likely because of its low initial concentration and partial consumption during electrolysis. High-resolution XPS spectra detailed surface functional groups in G-CDs (Fig. 3b-e). Peaks at 284.6, 285.3, and 286.4 eV corresponded to C-C/C = C, C-OH, and C-O/C-O-C, respectively [35]. Peaks at 398, 399.7, and 401.5 eV are corresponding to C = N-C, N-H, and N-(C)3, respectively. Peaks at 531.2, 532.6, and 536.1 eV correspond to C-O/N-O, C-OH/C-O-C, and O-C = O, respectively. Peaks at 708.5 and 721.4 eV represented Fe(II) [36], while peaks at 708.9 and 722.7 eV represented Fe(III), with proportions of 34.2 % and 65.8 % for Fe(II) and Fe(III), respectively. Based on the data, we can infer that the the electrolysis also causes the conversion of Fe(II) to Fe(III), doping the CDs.

The presence of Cr(VI) could decline the fluorescence at 510 nm obviously, and the property was explored for Cr(VI) quantitation. In order to enhance the sensitivity, optimizations were conducted on potential influencing factors such as pH, temperature, and time. The impact of pH on G-CDs fluorescence was investigated over the range of 5–12 upon the addition of 50 μM Cr(VI) (Fig. 4 a). Chrysoidine G can act as the pH indicator [37]; thus pH can affect the quenching efficiency by Cr(VI). We use ΔF/F0 to quantify the variation, where ΔF represents the difference in fluorescence intensity at 510 nm, and F0 denotes the intensity of the blank G-CDs at 510 nm. ΔF/F0 tends to decrease under extremely acidic or alkaline conditions. This can be attributed to the presence of numerous hydroxyl groups on the G-CDs surface. In acidic solutions, these hydroxyl groups may protonate, while in alkaline conditions, chemical bonds undergo deprotonation, leading to negatively charge [38]. This, in turn, causes G-CDs aggregation and fluorescence quenching. The maximum variation of ΔF/F0 is observed at pH 8.

G-CDs were subjected to reactions with 50 µM Cr(VI) at various temperatures (20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 °C). Fig. 4b shows that under excitation at 308 nm, ΔF/F0 increases as the temperature rises from 20 °C to 30 °C, followed by stabilization between 40 and 60 °C. Additionally, the time-dependent ΔF/F0 of G-CDs after the addition of 50 µM Cr(VI) was monitored at different temperatures (Fig. 4c). Interestingly, upon addition, G-CDs swiftly reacted with Cr(VI), causing a steep decline in fluorescence intensity. However, with an increase in time to 40 min, the fluorescence gradually recovered, ultimately stabilizing. Thus, pH 8, 30 °C, and 40 min were chosen for quantification.“

Under the optimal conditions, the fluorescence emission spectra for G-CDs in the presence of different Cr(VI) concentration were shown in Fig. 5 a. Within the Cr(VI) concentration range of 0.25–1000 µM, the emission intensity of the G-CD solution at 510 nm decreased as the concentration increased, with complete quenching observed at a concentration of 1000 μM. Correspondingly, the green fluorescence gradually diminished (Inset in Fig. 5b). Fig. 5b presents the standard curve plotting ΔF/F0 against Cr(VI) concentration. The results indicate linear correlation in the range of 0.25–100 μM, with the equation ΔF/F0 = 0.005 + 0.015 CCr(VI) and an R2 of 0.996. Under the optimal experimental conditions based on S/N = 3, the method exhibited a limit of detection (LOD) for Cr(VI) of 0.15 μM. The LOD is comparable to or better than that reported in many studies [21–23,39,40], enabling our assay with effective detection capacity of the samples below allowable limit. Also, some CDs were developed for Cr(VI) detection with a much lower LOD. However, their synthesis of CDs is via hydrothermal or microwave method, which is time and energy consuming. To date, we have achieved Cr(VI) detection for the first time using electrochemically synthesized CDs. Additionally, the set-up is quite simple, implying that the CDs can be prepared on-field for sample detection.

Selective interference tests were conducted under the same detection environment to assess the potential impact of various interfering metal ions. Fifteen metal ion solutions at 100 μM (Co2+, Fe3+, Li+, Zn2+, NH4 +, Cu2+, Sn2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Ag+, Mn2+, Cr3+, Ni2+, Cd2+, and Al3+) were applied for interference analysis. As shown in Fig. 6 , the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs remained relatively unchanged when reacting with other ions. This indicates that the CDs retain strong selectivity for Cr(VI) response.

To validate the practical applicability of the method, G-CDs were utilized for the detection and analysis of drinking water samples collected from our lab. The detection results are presented in Table 1 . Cr(VI) was not detected in the tested water samples, which conforms to the limit of 0.005 mg/kg for Cr(VI) content set by the Hygienic Standard for Drinking Water (GB 5749–2006). The detection of spiked samples shows the recovery rate of Cr(VI) ions ranged from 98.8 % to 100.1 %, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) not exceeding 2.1 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism for Cr(VI) sensing by G-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Further investigation was conducted to explore the fluorescence quenching mechanism of G-CDs in the presence of Cr(VI). FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 7 a) showed an increase in the peak at 1265 cm−1 corresponding to C-O [41] and C=O (1068 cm−1) [42]. The characteristic peaks at 1389 cm−1 were attributed to the vibration of amide III (C-N). The XPS total spectrum (Fig. 7b) exhibited signals at 290.3, 410.3, 545.3, 594.3, and 740.3 eV corresponding to C1s (11.22 %), N1s (2.98 %), O1s (83.29 %), Cr2p (1.63 %), and Fe2p (0.89 %), respectively. After reaction with Cr(VI), the proportion of O1s in G-CDs increased apparently, while the proportions of C1s and N1s decreased. Because Cr(VI) exist as the form of Cr2O7 2-, after interaction with G-CDs, the content of O1 s inevitably increased. The XPS confirmed the direction interaction between G-CDs an Cr2O7 2-. The detailed XPS of C1s, N1s, O1s, Fe2p, and Cr2p is shown in Fig. 7c-g. Further analysis of N1s showed peaks at 397.6, 398.5, and 399.9 eV corresponding to N-(C)3, N-H, and C=N, respectively. Peaks at 531.2, 532.6, and 536.1 eV were corresponding to C-O/N-O, C-OH/C-O-C, and O-C=O, respectively. The Cr2p3/2 and Cr2p1/2 signals were divided into four component bands. Peaks at 576.7 and 586.4 eV belonged to Cr(III), while those at 579.0 and 583.0 eV belonged to Cr(VI). The Cr(III):Cr(VI) ratio in content was 1:4, suggesting that Cr(VI) ions introduced in G-CDs were partially reduced to Cr(III) during the reaction by Fe(II) on G-CDs. The interaction between Fe(III) produced during electrolysis and hydroxyl groups on G-CDs formed Fe(III)–OH complexes, which could induce the generation of •OH and Fe(II) through ligand-to-metal charge transfer reactions, probably further causing fluorescence quenching [43].

Fluorescence lifetime is an effective means to determine the dynamic or static quenching mechanism [44]. Therefore, fluorescence decay curves of G-CDs with and without Cr(VI) were tested (Fig. 7h). The fluorescence lifetimes were 9.34 ns and 9.12 ns, respectively, showing no significant change and implying a static quenching mechanism of G-CDs by Cr(VI) [45]. In Fig. 7i, G-CDs exhibited an increase in particle size with increasing Cr(VI) concentration, indicating induced aggregation due to interactions between carboxyl and nitrogen functional groups on the CD surface. The Zeta potential of G-CDs shifted from a positive charge of 0.3 to −11.6 mV (Fig. 7j), implying opposite charges on G-CDs and dichromate ions is responsible for aggregation. Above all, the fluorescence quenching of G-CDs by Cr(VI) is via a static mechanism. The origin of the fluorescence was due to surface sate emission, affected by carbon bonds and chemical groups. The presence of Cr(VI) induced the aggregation and surface charge version of G-CDs, as well as the oxidization of chemical groups. As a result, the generation of photoelectrons was impeded, thereby restraining the production of fluorescence.

In conclusion, this study successfully synthesized G-CDs through electrolysis of a combination of chrysoidine G and potassium ferrocyanide for Cr(VI) detection. The use of potassium ferrocyanide can improve the optical performance of the G-CDs efficiently in contrast to those obtained without the use. The G-CDs exhibited strong green fluorescence with an excitation wavelength of 308 nm and emission wavelength of 510 nm. They had a fluorescence quantum yield of 6.05 % and an average particle size of 3 to 5 nm. The detection range for Cr(VI) was 0.25 to 100 µM, with a LOD of 0.15 μM. The G-CDs showed unique responsiveness to Cr(VI) and demonstrated high recovery rates in detecting Cr(VI) in water samples. Additionally, the quenching was confirmed to be via a static mechanism, with the redox reaction, nanoparticle aggregation, and surface charge change also playing a role.

Tiantian Long: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Hongmei Luo: Formal analysis, Methodology, Validation. Hongchen Li: Investigation, Methodology. Jingbo Sun: Investigation, Validation. Yang Wang: Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Jiaquan Zhou: Methodology, Resources. Yi Chen: Resources, Validation, Visualization. Dong Xu: Conceptualization, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by funding from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21505162, 31571874), Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province, China (2020RC3042, 2022SK2102), Hainan Province Science and Technology Special Fund 396 (Grant No. ZDYF2020114), Hunan Provincial Department of Education Scientific Research Project (240994), Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Safety Monitoring and Early Warning (No.2020KFJJ05), the Grain-Oil Process and Quality Control 2011 Collaborative and Innovative Grant from Hunan province, and Training Program for Excellent Young Innovators of Changsha (kq1802043). We alsogratefully acknowledge financial support from China Scholarship Council.
